1: ὑπηρέτας Χριστοῦ (servants of Christ) — hypēretēs was originally the under-rower on a trireme (the lowest rank of rowers below the main deck), then generalized to mean subordinate assistant. It conveys harder, more subordinate labor than diakonos. Οἰκονόμους μυστηρίων θεοῦ (stewards of the mysteries of God) — the oikonomos was a slave-manager trusted with the master's household and finances; responsible, trusted, but accountable to the master alone, not to the household.
2: ζητεῖται ἐν τοῖς οἰκονόμοις ἵνα πιστός τις εὑρεθῇ (what is sought in stewards is that a faithful person be found) — present passive zēteitai: the ongoing requirement. Pistos (faithful/trustworthy) returns to the root of pistis; the steward's virtue is not eloquence or wisdom but reliability in the master's service. The passive heurethē (be found [faithful]) anticipates the eschatological evaluation of 4:5.
3: εἰς ἐλάχιστόν ἐστιν (it is a very little thing to me) — the superlative of mikros: it is the very smallest matter imaginable. Paul relativizes the Corinthian judgment to near-zero. Ἀνθρωπίνης ἡμέρας (human day) — a wordplay on hēmera kyriou (the Day of the Lord): the Corinthians are holding a human day (a human tribunal), not the divine Day.
4: οὐδὲν γὰρ ἐμαυτῷ σύνοιδα (for I am not conscious of anything against myself) — synoida (to be a co-witness with oneself, to be aware of as a shared knowledge) is a self-knowledge term: Paul's own conscience is clear. But the double negation qualifies this immediately: all ouk en toutō dedikaiōmai (but I have not been justified by this). Clean conscience ≠ divine acquittal; the competent judge is not Paul himself.
5: κρίνετε μηδὲν πρὸ καιροῦ (judge nothing before the time) — present imperative: stop the ongoing practice of judging. Heōs an elthē ho kyrios (until the Lord comes) — aorist subjunctive in a temporal clause; the judgment belongs to the Parousia. The Lord will phōtizei (illuminate) what is hidden and phanerōsei (manifest, make visible) the purposes of hearts — two future indicatives for the eschatological disclosure that renders present judgments premature.
6: μετεσχημάτισα εἰς ἐμαυτὸν καὶ Ἀπολλῶν (I have transferred [these things] in figure to myself and Apollos) — metaschēmatizō (to transform the form/figure of) indicates that Paul has been using himself and Apollos as representative figures for a broader principle. The actual targets of the criticism are other leaders or factions in Corinth. Τὸ μὴ ὑπὲρ ἃ γέγραπται (the not beyond what stands written) — the cryptic phrase may be an early proverbial rule about scriptural limits; possibly: do not go beyond what scripture has set down in your evaluations of teachers.
7: τί δὲ ἔχεις ὃ οὐκ ἔλαβες (but what do you have that you did not receive?) — the rhetorical question delivers the demolition: all gifts are received, not achieved. Ει δε και ελαβες, τί καυχᾶσαι ως μὴ λαβών (and if you did receive, why do you boast as though you had not received?) — the aorist elabes and present kauchāsai create an irony: the reception was completed; the boasting continues as if it were not.
8: ἤδη κεκορεσμένοι ἐστέ (already you are satiated/stuffed) — the perfect passive kekoresmenoi: they have achieved a state of satiation and remain in it. The irony is thick: the Corinthians act as if they have already arrived at eschatological fullness. Ἐβασιλεύσατε (you began to reign) — aorist: they inaugurated their reign. Chōris hēmōn (without us): without the apostles who are still suffering. Paul's apostolic hardship catalogue (vv.9-13) will make the contrast devastating.
9: ἐπιθανατίους (epithanatious = condemned to die) — a hapax legomenon in Paul (only here in NT): those condemned to death who were exhibited last in the gladiatorial procession for maximum dramatic effect. The spectacle (theatron) is the same word as theater: Paul and the apostles are exhibited as a theatrical spectacle to the cosmos, angels, and humans. The triadic audience (cosmos, angels, humans) is cosmic in scope.
10: ἡμεῖς μωροὶ διὰ Χριστόν (we are fools because of Christ) — the emphatic pronoun hēmeis (we apostles) contrasts with the implied hymeis (you Corinthians) in each antithesis: we are fools — you are wise; we are weak — you are strong; we are without honor — you are honored. Each antithesis is sarcastic: the Corinthians have claimed what the apostles have not yet received, demonstrating their realized-eschatology confusion.
11: ἄχρι τῆς ἄρτι ὥρας (until this very hour) — the precision is temporal: not in the past, not hypothetically, but to this present moment. The apostolic hardship list (hungry, thirsty, in rags, brutally treated, homeless) is present reality, not rhetorical hyperbole. The list serves as evidence against the Corinthian triumphalism: the apostolic paradigm is current, not historical.
12: κοπιῶμεν ἐργαζόμενοι ταῖς ἰδίαις χερσίν (we labor working with our own hands) — present participle: ongoing manual labor, which in the Greek social hierarchy was the activity of the lower classes and artisans, not of the educated. Paul deliberately places himself in the social class below the Corinthians who valorize wisdom and status. The behavioral responses (eulogoumen... anechometha... parakaloumen — we bless... we endure... we gently answer) move from verb to verb with asyndeton, each a present tense response pattern.
13: περικαθάρματα τοῦ κόσμου ἐγενήθημεν (we became the scum of the world) — aorist passive: the state they were brought into. Perikatharmata (scrapings around the outside, filth cleaned off) was used for the waste matter scraped from a pot and also for persons devoted as scapegoat-sacrifices to cleanse a city from pollution. Peripsēma (refuse, off-scrapings) reinforces: the apostolic ministry places them at the absolute bottom of social status.
14: ἐντρέπων γράφω ταῦτα (I am not writing these things to shame you) — present active: the ongoing purpose of the letter is parental correction, not public humiliation. The distinction between entrepō (shame as goal) and noutheteō (admonish, correct) is between punitive and corrective intent. Paul positions himself as father, not adversary.
15: ἐν γὰρ Χριστῷ Ἰησοῦ διὰ τοῦ εὐαγγελίου ἐγὼ ὑμᾶς ἐγέννησα (for in Christ Jesus through the gospel I became your father) — aorist: a completed act. The prepositional chain locates the fatherhood in Christ and through the specific instrument of the gospel. Paul's unique claim as father is based on his role as the one who first proclaimed the gospel that brought the community into being — not on ongoing pastoral relationship, which anyone can provide.
16: μιμηταί μου γίνεσθε (become imitators of me) — present imperative: begin and keep on becoming. Mimētēs (imitator) carries the sense of modeling and reproducing a pattern, not blind copying. The imitation is not of Paul's personality but of his pattern of cruciform living (the paradigm of vv.9-13). This call to imitation will be grounded in 11:1 with kathōs kagō Christou (as I am of Christ).
17: ἀνέμνησεν ὑμᾶς (will remind you) — future active: Timothy's mission is to bring the Corinthians back to what they already know. The object: tas hodous mou tas en Christō (my ways in Christ) — not merely Paul's apostolic teaching but his way of living, his pattern of conduct. Kathōs pantachou en pasē ekklēsia didaskō (as I teach everywhere in every church): the appeal to universally consistent teaching delegitimizes Corinthian exceptionalism.
18: ἐφυσιώθησάν τινες (some have become puffed up) — aorist passive: they were inflated into a state of pride. Phusioō (to inflate, to puff up — from physa, bellows) will appear six more times in 1 Corinthians (4:19; 5:2; 8:1; 13:4; twice in 4:18-19): the letter's running diagnosis of what is wrong in Corinth is arrogant inflation, the opposite of the cruciform deflation Paul models.
19: γνώσομαι οὐ τὸν λόγον τῶν πεφυσιωμένων ἀλλὰ τὴν δύναμιν (I will know not the speech of those who are puffed up but the power) — future indicative: a personal declaration of Paul's evaluative criterion when he arrives. The contrast logos (speech, rhetoric) versus dynamis (power) recapitulates the entire argument of chapters 1-4: rhetorical eloquence is not the currency of divine action.
20: οὐ γὰρ ἐν λόγῳ ἡ βασιλεία τοῦ θεοῦ ἀλλ᾽ ἐν δυνάμει (for the kingdom of God is not in speech but in power) — the present tense is definitive. Basileia tou theou (kingdom of God) appears rarely in 1 Corinthians (4:20; 6:9-10; 15:24, 50); its presence here concludes the wisdom-cross-power argument with the eschatological reference: the age-to-come order operates on different criteria than this age.
21: ἐν ῥάβδῳ ἔλθω πρὸς ὑμᾶς ἢ ἐν ἀγάπῃ πνεύματί τε πραΰτητος (shall I come with a rod or in love and a spirit of gentleness?) — the rhabdos (rod, staff) is the instrument of parental correction in Prov 13:24. The triad agapē (love), pneuma (spirit), praütēs (gentleness) characterizes the preferred mode of the apostolic visit. The choice Paul offers is not between wrath and indifference but between two modes of fatherly presence — correctional or gentle — depending on the community's response before he arrives.
